svg.text: Scaling OTLP Traces to 1,000,000+ Signals / Minute
Two tiers. No proxy. No Kafka. The load-balancing exporter is the only correct fan-out.

g.text: ▸ gRPC :4317 — HTTP/2, binary protobuf, multiplexed
✓ ~20–30% less bandwidth · lower CPU → default at scale
▸ HTTP :4318 — simple, debuggable, any LB works
✗ gRPC = 1 long-lived conn → L4 LB pins it to
one backend (the whole problem starts here)
Verdict: gRPC for throughput; pinning is the catch.
▸ The trace_id lives inside the protobuf body
✗ One OTLP request = a BATCH of many traces
✗ One header can’t represent N trace_ids
✗ Proxy sends whole batch to 1 backend →
N−1 traces land on the wrong gateway
Works only for service.name (batch-constant).
▸ BatchSpanProcessor groups by time/size
— never by trace
✓ Amortizes network + compression → vital at 1M/min
▸ Concurrency means every batch mixes traces
✗ 1 trace / request → per-trace RPC, throughput
collapses. So routing must split the batch.

svg.text: REFERENCE ARCHITECTURE · TWO TIERS · 1M SIGNALS / MIN
one tier does collect + route — no L7 LB, no router tier
The agent IS the router. It decodes each batch, splits by trace_id, and scatters — so every span of a trace reaches the same gateway (1 request → N backends).
HOW MULTIPLE PODS AGREE · CONVERGENT COMPUTATION, NOT COORDINATION
The pods never talk to each other — they compute the same answer.
same gateway list → same ring → same node
Shared input = the gateway list (from the resolver) — NOT the trace IDs. Same list ⇒ every pod independently lands trace T on gw-1. Zero messaging, no SPOF.
WHY NOT A NORMAL LOAD BALANCER?

g.text: ✗ Picks backend once at connect, pins forever
✗ gRPC multiplexes on 1 conn → 1 collector hot,
the rest idle. You “scaled” but balanced nothing.
Fine for OTLP/HTTP only. Never for gRPC at scale.
✓ Balances per request → even connection spread
✗ Trace-blind: can’t read trace_id in protobuf
✗ Can’t split a batch (1 req → 1 upstream)
Distributes load — but never keeps a trace whole.
✓ The only thing that decodes → splits → scatters
✓ Same trace_id → same gateway, deterministically
✓ Stateless · runs on every node → scales for free
No proxy to deploy. This is the answer.

svg.text: THE EFFICIENT MINIMUM · & WHAT TO WATCH
Tier 1 — two valid forms (pick by need)
✓ you also collect logs · host metrics · k8s enrichment
✓ traces-only · enrichment already in the SDK
→ fewer pods · central · Service = discovery, not L4 pin
Both: stateless · run the loadbalancing exporter · deterministic hash →
no SPOF · persistent queue + retry = durability, no Kafka.
How trace IDs are shared & what to watch

g.text: ▸ Trace IDs aren’t broadcast — they’re hashed.
Same id → same gateway, computed independently.
✗ Rebalancing churn: gateway set changes → ~R/N
routes remap (bigger N = smaller blast radius)
✗ Gateways need stable identity (StatefulSet + headless)
✓ routing_key: traceID for tail-sampling · service for spanmetrics
resolver: k8s / dns at the gateway headless service.

svg.text: Don’t add a proxy. Don’t add Kafka. Add a routing_key.
The agent you already run can keep every trace whole — decode, split, scatter by trace_id. Two stateless tiers, scaled horizontally,
and the SPOF you feared and the broker you dreaded both quietly disappear.
